I think we should consider adding a layer of indirection (leaning in to / fleshing out [1])  to leave us room to support some cases we've repeatedly discussed:
* sending signed s3 urls to browser and removing webservers as middle man for bytes
* having "captured logs" just generate urls to existing capture systems 
* streaming (maybe already covered)

just riffing - some tagged type w/ enum or union of supported types, something like:
*  "direct" and gives back the `key` that you can pass back to dagit to get bytes directly
* "indirect_url" where a url is returned that we can download to in browser
* "indirect_external" where a url or just display string is given to show in dagit to help the user get to existing logs but we dont deal with bytes directiy 

dont love those names, but i think as long as we leave a layer of indirection via tagged types and make the APIs somewhat coherent against that we will be setup

The two conventions for module-level variables I see in pep8 are:

[Global variable names](https://peps.python.org/pep-0008/#global-variable-names):

> (Let’s hope that these variables are meant for use inside one module only.) The conventions are about the same as those for functions.

> Modules that are designed for use via from M import * should use the __all__ mechanism to prevent exporting globals, or use the older convention of prefixing such globals with an underscore (which you might want to do to indicate these globals are “module non-public”).

and [Constants](https://peps.python.org/pep-0008/#constants):

> Constants are usually defined on a module level and written in all capital letters with underscores separating words. Examples include MAX_OVERFLOW and TOTAL.

What do you think about making instances  either `_instances` or `INSTANCES` (and same for registry).

As a user of the high-level API, these changes all look very sensible to me. I cannot see any argument that is made keyword-only here which I would rather provide as a positional argument; doing so would be compromising readability of the code.

When it comes to reading code, I would personally prefer also seeing `expressions` and `cut` written as kwarg (the latter more so than the former). Users are of course still free to do so.

Another approach to determine where positional-only arguments should start could be making anything that has a default value positional-only. I do not see a significant gain in that approach compared to the current implementation though.

For my tests, I stopped using spies that record and for which you assert the behaviour later,
 and instead I am using and ReplayAgentBacked similar to the angularjs `$httpBackend`

You pass a list of expected URLs and request data, but you also able to return a response.
In this way, you can test chained agent interactions, without touching the network.

For example, I am using for SAML login testing in which more than 1 requests are made...and the response of the first request is used in the second  request.

- If a SRP client switches to another server, the origianl server, if running, still has the SRP services. The SRP client may fail to register on the new server due to probing failures.

I feel here the API is **not consistent** with the doc.

The idea here is that: `Workflow[T]`  is more like a lift of `ObjectRef[T]`.

`def run()` will do unlifting: transform workflow -> objectref

`def step()` will do lifting: transfer a func's return objectref -> workflow


As for the concept in this PR, if I understand it correctly:
```
Workflow --> WorkflowTask
WorkflowStepResult --> Workflow
```

"""

I'm mostly in favor of this change.

The one change I'm most concerned about is:

>     * Removed the Words() class, because:
>       
>       * the weird way in which it was subclassing str was busting type checking, and fixing that was basically redoing the class
>       * with only four properties and no methods, it doesn't really rise to the occasion of its own class
>       * While working through the conversion, I found that the author was at times confusingly clobbering the contents of the words object. Only a careful third party would pick up on this, and using separate variables for separate values is vastly more readable.
>       * The class wasn't really doing its job great. The author clearly meant this to cache some frequently-used transformations, but this left him reluctant to add more than the original four transformations to the class. By tearing apart the class and just using local variables in scope I was able to pick up some more places that could re-use string modifications.

There are a lot of different concerns here. I was unaware that subclassing str would interfere with type checking. It really should not, as `Word()` is a `str`. Moreover, I did appreciate how this class would encapsulate and avoid repetition around common behaviors for words. It's worthwhile to describe a common behavior once than copy/paste it throughout already complicated functions.

I appreciate that you separated most concerns into separate commits, but this commit happens to be squashed with the typechecking change, so reverting it or rebasing without it would lose a lot of other work.

I much prefer to see `word.first` than `first`, as the former provides more context and allows for more than one `first`.

I don't doubt you've discovered clobbering, but that seems like a bug that should be fixed in a separate commit. As written, it's difficult to see where the clobbering was happening or what harm that might cause.

As I think about it more, the value from these other changes exceeds the value lost from the Word class, so perhaps I'll just accept it as is and as I have time and energy, I might bring it back (or something similar).

Thanks for all the work on this!

I'm not sure I'd like to endorse this approach officially. I'm also uneasy exposing the internal API of the backend. I'd prefer instead to provide supported hooks for this purpose.

Although setup_requires is deprecated for installing packages, it's not deprecated for the PEP 517 build backend. e.g.:

```
# setup.py

import setuptools
import random

setuptools.setup(setup_requires=[random.choice(['dep_a', 'dep_b'])])
```

Of course, this change doesn't give selectability between dependencies for sdist and binary builds, that I'd like to support. For that, I'd like to provide `build_requires` or similar, with support for static builds and dynamic ones.

My worry about this documentation is that it elevates the technique and encourages adopting of the approach, but I'd like to reserve this approach for very selective use-cases.

I really appreciate the work here, but I'm still leaning away from it.

Since the alternative is to let it linger, perhaps for a long time, I'm going to go ahead and accept the change, but I'm going to suggest we make the support "provisional" by making it clear that this approach is not recommended for any use case, but is available for those that absolutely need it. WDYT?

I appreciate the refactor into multiple files!  There is certainly room for further improvements to the codebase in this service, but this is a nice start.

I think the scopes question is an important one.  The GitHub service automatically has `assume:repo:github.com/*`, so whatever scopes `queue:rerun-task:...` that implies will apply to re-running a task as this PR is currently written -- which is probably both too broad (one project can rerun another project's tasks) and too narrow (if no role has the necessary `queue:rerun-task:..` scope, the rerun will fail).

The situation is both a little bit risky, because _lots_ of people can click that rerun button in GitHub, and a little bit safe, because it's difficult to forge a GitHub webhook and even more difficult to forge a pulse message, so it's reasonable to assume that the suite/run ID's are something the originating user had access to.

Today I looked closer at both the changes in this PR and also the preexisting code and a few things raised some flags.

I went back to check [the Figma spec](https://www.figma.com/file/uiZuCIqh2KYvBUfbCiJyyA/Hybrid-Learning?node-id=76%3A6747), and unfortunately it isn't explicit about how the grid layout within the card is supposed to work. This is something that future KDS reviews should hopefully help address.

Looking at it though, my impression was that the intent is something like this:

![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/2367265/146200859-a97ff6ac-1cde-41e6-986e-e4c352368015.png)

However the actual DOM structure looks like this:

![2021-12-15 08 45 59](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/2367265/146201031-ec2f4983-4748-41dc-add5-d00d8702c600.gif)

One serious user-facing consequence of this is that the whole bottom-half of the card looks like it should be clickable but is not. This will cause especially tablet users a ton of frustration:

![2021-12-15 09 00 57](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/2367265/146201197-c4ac1a10-ef0c-4875-afd5-6bf3cef136cf.gif)

I think the overall layout of this component needs to be cleaned up. If it would be helpful, I'd be happy to take this on in order to demonstrate how `KGrid` can be useful here.

@avnishn let's take the marl_loss_and_optim.py out of this PR for now. Let's complete the feature set of RLoptimizer with complete docstrings, unittests, its torch extension, and an example ppo RLOptimizer with which you write your unittests with. Then we will add `marl_loss_and_optim.py` (Also let's call that marl_optimizer.py and its class MultiAgentRLOptimizer) so in `core/rl_optimizer/`, we have `rl_optimizer.py -> RLOptimizer` and `marl_optimizer.py -> RLOptimizer`.

The things that are missing from `rl_optimizer.py`: 
- How would user define the actual gradient optimizers? I remember we decided on some abstractmethod called `get_optimizer()`?
- How would the caller work with this class? What are the sequence of calls?
- Example PPOTorchRLOptimizer class. 
- Unittest and documentation. 

Clarifying the design here:
```
class RLOptimizer:

	def configure_optimizers():
		pass

	def compute_loss():
		pass

	def on_before_compute_loss():
		pass

	def on_after_compute_loss():
		pass

###### NOTE: this is not in the docstring format, please modify it to a more concise form for the purpose of the docstring. Please also don't read too much in the name of attributes and methods that I picked here. We can finalize the names once the functionality is fully fleshed out. 

###### also note that all this code is getting run and constructed on a single process

class RLTrainerActor:

	def __init__():
		# the single/multi agent Module that is constructed inside the RLTrainer
		self.module = ...
		# the module is passed by reference to the optimizer
		self.rl_optimizer = RLOptimizer(self.module, ...)
		# an alias to the optimizers object created after we invoke this method
		self.optimizers = self.rl_optimizer.configure_optimizers()

	def update(fwd_in, **kwargs):
		fwd_in: SampleBatchType = ...

		# if module is MARLModule sample will be MABatch and the output will also be in MA format
		fwd_out = self.module.forward_train(fwd_in=sample)
		###### ----> This is where the on_before_compute_loss() could go
		loss_out = self.rl_optimizer.compute_loss(fwd_in, fwd_out)
		###### ----> This is where on_after_compute_loss() could go and so on. 
		self.rl_optimizer.log_and_compile(fwd_in, fwd_out, loss_out)
		grads = self.compute_gradients(loss_out)
		self.apply_gradients(grads)

	def compute_gradients(loss_out):
		# for torch
		loss_out["loss_total"].backward()
		grads = {n: p.grad for n, p in self.module.named_parameters()}
		return grads

	def apply_gradients(grads):
		# for torch
		self.optimizers.step()

# the RLTrainerActor will itself be managed by the TrainerRunner which will coordinate the RLTrainerActors and which 
module they will be responsible for. But RLTrainerActor's code does not care whether the underlying module is a 
MARLModule or RLModule or whether the rl_optimizer is RLOptimizer or MARLOptimizer. The API and functionality looks the 
same. 


```

I'm not sure this is what we want per default.

For instance, with this change, if a Tune run is stopped and I restore and want to continue it, my training will completely fail and it will be impossible for me to resume at all, as the wandb IDs already exist and I can't override the stored setting.

I think this setting is trying to solve a problem for the wrong reasons. The original issue was that duplicate trial IDs were generated when starting parallel new experiments, leading to conflicts. We've since improved the trial generation (#29900) so the problem shouldn't come up anymore. And if it did, the correct recommendation here should be that the user should just pass `resume="never"` to their wandb config.


Let's consider the journeys and requirements real quick.

1. I start a run. I expect each run to be logged separately to wandb.

This generally worked and was improved by #29900. For additional guarantees, users can choose to pass `resume="never"`.

2. I start and stop a run. I resume the run. I expect the resumed run to be logged to wandb.

This works at the moment because we just overwrite existing runs per default. With the current PR, this wouldn't work anymore, users would have to pass `resume=None` manually. But this is a far more common case than the problem we encountered in 1.

3. I start and stop a run. I resume the run. I expect the resumed run to be logged to wandb but _not to lose previously logged data_.

This is a bit more tricky, as we may resume from an earlier point in time, so the results in wandb could look a bit funky. I think it may be worthwhile exploring this option. However, so far we haven't seen any user requests for this.

To look into this, we should probably try it out ourselves - how do the results look like with stopping/resuming at the moment (and overwriting) - does this only overwrite local results or also online results? What is the difference if we stop/resume with `resume="auto"`?

Generally I think the current default is sensible as it covers the most common use cases gracefully and we didn't run into problems with the resume behavior so far (other than what we fixed in #29900). But very open to discussions here.
